skill.name: sequenzy
skill.description: Guide agents through Sequenzy CLI and MCP workflows for email marketing, subscribers, lists, segments, campaigns, sequences, webhooks, products, teams, inbox tasks, and API keys.
skill.summary: Use Sequenzy CLI and MCP workflows with clear command selection, safety checks, and operational caveats.
skill.author: Sequenzy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review shows the files are Markdown guidance and YAML metadata, not executable code. The real risks are legitimate Sequenzy CLI execution, network API calls, environment API key handling, and destructive operations that can bypass confirmations. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Run CLI Commands Can Mutate Sequenzy State
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to run the Sequenzy CLI for login, account checks, subscriber management, campaign work, list changes, sequence enrollment, webhooks, and product delivery. This is expected functionality, but it can change external Sequenzy account data when used without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are documented Markdown, not embedded executable code, so command injection is not confirmed. Confidence is high that agents using the skill will execute real CLI commands that can mutate remote data.

medium_findings.title: Network API And Webhook Operations Are Expected But Sensitive
medium_findings.description: The references document Sequenzy API requests, default service URLs, device auth polling, and webhook creation or replay. These operations can send business data, subscriber information, and webhook test events to configured services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicit and tied to the Sequenzy product workflow. No unauthorized endpoint or exfiltration pattern was found, but the capability handles sensitive account data.

medium_findings.title: API Key And One-Time Secret Handling Requires Care
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents to use SEQUENZY_API_KEY for automation and to surface one-time API keys or webhook signing secrets to the user. This is legitimate, but careless logging or transcript sharing could expose credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential references are clearly operational guidance rather than secret harvesting. The risk remains real because the workflow creates and displays sensitive values.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Operations May Skip Confirmation
medium_findings.description: Several documented commands use --yes or note actions that skip prompts, including list, tag, segment, webhook, invitation, and campaign lifecycle operations. This raises accidental deletion or cancellation risk if the agent does not confirm intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive behavior is explicitly documented and has clear operational impact. It is not malicious, but it requires user confirmation before use.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives From Markdown Syntax
low_findings.description: Most Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and hardcoded URL flags are Markdown inline code, route examples, option names, IDs, or ordinary documentation. Review found no executable Ruby, shell backtick code, weak cryptographic implementation, or prompt injection attempt.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown and YAML documentation, and the suspicious tokens appear inside fenced commands, inline code, or normal product terms. No runnable implementation code is present in this skill package.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs agents to run Sequenzy CLI commands. Treat these as user-authorized operational commands and inspect before mutation where possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented examples rather than code executed by the skill itself. The risk is agent-mediated command execution against a real service.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Environment Variable Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends SEQUENZY_API_KEY for non-interactive automation. Agents should avoid printing or storing this value outside the intended environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable use is explicit and legitimate. It still handles a bearer-style secret that must be protected.

dangerous_patterns.title: Confirmation Bypass Flags
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes --yes for destructive commands and says some cancellation commands skip prompts. Agents should confirm target IDs and user intent before using these flags.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The bypass pattern is real and intentionally documented for non-TTY environments. The risk is accidental or overbroad mutation, not hidden malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Sequenzy has many CLI and MCP workflows, and choosing the wrong command can change live marketing data. This skill helps agents pick supported commands, verify inputs, and surface caveats before acting.
actual_capabilities: Select supported Sequenzy CLI commands for account, company, subscriber, list, tag, segment, template, campaign, sequence, team, inbox, webhook, website, product, API key, and transactional email tasks.
Explain when to use CLI workflows, MCP equivalents, dashboard URLs, or direct API use.
Guide authentication through interactive login or the SEQUENZY_API_KEY environment variable.
Identify unsupported or placeholder workflows, including campaign immediate send.
Call out operational caveats for destructive commands, one-time secrets, campaign tests, sequence generation, and product delivery files.
Reference dashboard URL patterns for campaigns, sequences, templates, companies, and settings tabs.
limitations: The skill is a guide and does not include executable source code or a bundled Sequenzy CLI.
It depends on the installed Sequenzy CLI or MCP tools matching the documented implementation.
It cannot verify live account permissions, campaign status, or API responses without running external tools.
It should defer specialized email marketing tasks to the narrower sequenzy-email-marketing skill when available.

use_cases.title: Prepare a campaign workflow
use_cases.description: Choose the right campaign, template, list, and scheduling commands while checking review URLs and unsupported send-now behavior.

use_cases.title: Automate subscriber operations
use_cases.description: Use list, tag, segment, and sequence enrollment commands with batching rules, filters, and dry-run guidance where supported.

use_cases.title: Manage operational integrations
use_cases.description: Inspect API keys, webhooks, websites, products, team members, and inbox workflows with clear credential and deletion precautions.

prompt_templates.title: Check Authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Sequenzy skill to check whether this machine is authenticated. Tell me which command to run and what the result means.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Subscriber Import
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Sequenzy skill to plan a subscriber import into a named list from a CSV file. Include validation steps and safe batching notes.

prompt_templates.title: Review Campaign Scheduling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Sequenzy skill to prepare a draft campaign for scheduling. Include the commands, required IDs, review URL behavior, and unsupported alternatives.

prompt_templates.title: Design Sequence Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Sequenzy skill to update a sequence with branch conditions and then enroll selected subscribers. Include risks, required files, and verification steps.

output_examples.input: I need to add a CSV of leads to the VIP list and tag them.
output_examples.output: The response identifies list and tag commands, asks for the list ID or name, checks CSV email format, recommends inspecting existing tags, and warns before any mutation.

output_examples.input: Create a webhook for bounced and complained email events.
output_examples.output: The response names the webhook create command, lists required URL and event inputs, notes test and delivery inspection commands, and warns that the signing secret appears once.

output_examples.input: Stop enrollments in a sequence for customers with matching order IDs.
output_examples.output: The response chooses cancel-enrollments with field values, recommends a dry run first, explains when --apply is needed, and asks for the sequence ID and field path.

best_practices: Inspect account, company, IDs, counts, and preview URLs before running mutations.
Use SEQUENZY_API_KEY only in trusted automation environments and avoid printing secret values.
Confirm target IDs and user intent before delete, cancel, replay, invite, or --yes operations.
anti_patterns: Do not invent Sequenzy commands that are not wired in the CLI or MCP implementation.
Do not use destructive commands from examples without confirming the exact target and impact.
Do not paste API keys, webhook signing secrets, or subscriber files into unrelated tools or logs.

faq.question: Does this skill run Sequenzy commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance for agents that can run the installed Sequenzy CLI or available MCP tools.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: When should I use SEQUENZY_API_KEY?
faq.answer: Use it for trusted automation or CI. Interactive local work can use sequenzy login instead.

faq.question: Can it send a campaign immediately?
faq.answer: No. The skill says immediate campaign send is unsupported and recommends near-future scheduling instead.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation, but it guides real CLI, network, credential, and destructive account operations.

faq.question: What should agents verify before mutations?
faq.answer: Agents should verify authentication, company context, target IDs, recipient data, and user approval for destructive actions.
